content.assessment: T4 is the proposal most directly framed in education-finance terms: it proposes targeted compensatory funding to feeder/disadvantaged schools precisely because capitation financing currently strips them of both student revenue and peer effects with no compensatory mechanism (F-live-education_finance-05), and it targets the severe disadvantaged-skill gap (12.1% vs 16.3% EU average, F-live-education_finance-07) through resource reallocation rather than structural change. Given Hungary's low overall spending share and instructional time (F-live-education_finance-06), such reallocation could yield high marginal value for money since baseline provision is thin. However, it leaves cost incidence from tracking-driven sorting fully in place, meaning compensatory spending is an ongoing transfer rather than a one-time fix, with unclear scale of budget required relative to the depth of the disadvantaged-skill gap.
opportunities: Existing teacher-career/incentive funding channels (referenced qualitatively) could be redirected toward disadvantaged-school retention with lower marginal implementation cost than new structural reform
strengths: Directly targets the specific, evidenced funding/peer-effect loss mechanism affecting feeder schools under capitation financing (F-live-education_finance-05)
Addresses a severe and quantified resource-need gap: only 12.1% of disadvantaged students reach basic skill proficiency vs 16.3% EU average (F-live-education_finance-07)

Low baseline spending share (4.1%, rank 41/42) and instructional time (rank 32/34) mean marginal reallocation toward disadvantaged schools may have outsized value-for-money relative to systems with more fiscal slack (F-live-education_finance-06)
threats: If compensatory funding is set below the scale of the structural loss documented in F-live-education_finance-05, it may function as a symbolic rather than substantive offset, especially given the system's overall low spending capacity (F-live-education_finance-06)
weaknesses: As a compensatory rather than structural fix, funding must be sustained indefinitely to offset an ongoing sorting mechanism, implying recurring rather than one-time fiscal commitment
No cost estimate provided for the scale of targeted investment needed to meaningfully close the 12.1%/16.3% gap
